skill.name: mobile-security-coder
skill.description: Guides secure mobile application coding for iOS, Android, and cross-platform projects, with focus on storage, WebView, authentication, and network controls.
skill.summary: Apply secure mobile coding patterns across app storage, WebViews, authentication, networking, and privacy.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings are mostly false positives caused by security vocabulary in a defensive mobile secure-coding skill. No executable code, credential access, browser storage access, or shell execution was found in the scanned file. The remaining concern is dual-use guidance around obfuscation, anti-tampering, and dynamic analysis prevention.

medium_findings.title: Dual-Use Mobile Hardening Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill discusses code obfuscation, anti-tampering, anti-reverse engineering, packing, and dynamic analysis prevention. These are legitimate mobile app hardening topics, but they can also help malicious apps resist inspection.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text clearly includes dual-use anti-analysis topics, but the surrounding context frames them as defensive mobile application hardening.

low_findings.title: False Positive: External Command Execution
low_findings.description: The flagged line tells the assistant to open a referenced playbook file if examples are needed. It is not a Ruby backtick, shell command, or executable instruction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains plain Markdown text and a file reference. No shell syntax, interpreter call, or command execution path is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Storage and Keychain Security Terms
low_findings.description: The browser storage, sensitive storage, and Keychain findings are topic descriptions for secure mobile data storage. They do not access local storage, cookies, Keychain, Keystore, or credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is descriptive security guidance only. There is no code that reads browser storage, OS keychains, or application secrets.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts point to prose about secure mobile coding and the distinction between coding and auditing. No weak cryptographic algorithm is named or implemented.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines contain descriptive text, not cryptographic code or algorithm recommendations. No MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak primitive is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Pattern
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alerts are references to Android, iOS, platform security models, and obfuscation tools. They do not collect host information or inspect the user's environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines name mobile platform concepts in a knowledge list. There is no command, script, API call, or instruction to inventory a real machine.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Jailbreak Detection Keywords
low_findings.description: The jailbreak keyword findings describe defensive mobile controls for device security validation and graceful degradation. They are not prompt injection text or malware instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The terms appear in defensive mobile security sections and example prompts. The dual-use aspect is real, but there is no evidence of malicious intent in these lines.

content.value_statement: Mobile apps often expose sensitive data through storage, WebViews, authentication flows, and weak network settings. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code apply practical secure coding patterns for mobile projects.
actual_capabilities: Guides secure storage choices for iOS Keychain, Android Keystore, encrypted files, and local databases.
Reviews WebView settings for URL allowlisting, JavaScript controls, CSP, cookies, and file access restrictions.
Helps design mobile authentication using biometrics, MFA, OAuth PKCE, JWT handling, and session controls.
Recommends network security controls such as HTTPS enforcement, certificate validation, and certificate pinning.
Covers privacy, permissions, deep links, push notifications, and cross-platform mobile security patterns.
Provides mobile-focused review checklists aligned with OWASP MASVS concepts.
limitations: It does not run mobile security scanners or inspect compiled app binaries.
It cannot prove compliance without project evidence, test results, and human review.
Some hardening guidance is dual-use and should be limited to legitimate applications.
Platform APIs and store policies can change, so current official documentation should be checked.

use_cases.title: Harden a Mobile Feature
use_cases.description: Design secure storage, authentication, network, and permission controls before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Review WebView Risk
use_cases.description: Check WebView configuration for unsafe JavaScript, file access, bridge exposure, and weak URL validation.

use_cases.title: Prepare Release Security Notes
use_cases.description: Create a practical checklist for privacy, secure storage, network protection, and mobile threat coverage.

prompt_templates.title: Review One Mobile Feature
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a planned feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this mobile feature for security risks. Focus on data storage, permissions, authentication, network calls, and user privacy. Give prioritized fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Secure a WebView
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me configure a secure mobile WebView. Include URL allowlisting, JavaScript policy, bridge safety, cookie handling, CSP, and file access restrictions.

prompt_templates.title: Design Secure Token Storage
prompt_templates.prompt: Design secure token storage and refresh handling for a mobile app. Cover iOS, Android, biometric fallback, session timeout, and offline behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Release Security Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a mobile security release checklist for this app architecture. Include OWASP MASVS-aligned checks, tests, evidence to collect, and release blockers.

output_examples.input: Review my WebView design for a banking app.
output_examples.output: A prioritized review covering trusted origins, disabled default JavaScript, bridge restrictions, secure cookies, CSP, cache cleanup, and test cases.

output_examples.input: Plan secure local storage for refresh tokens.
output_examples.output: A platform-specific storage plan using Keychain or Keystore, biometric protection, rotation rules, timeout behavior, and recovery constraints.

output_examples.input: Create a release checklist for Android and iOS.
output_examples.output: A concise checklist for permissions, secure networking, storage, logging, deep links, privacy consent, dependency review, and validation evidence.

best_practices: Provide platform, framework, data types, threat model, and existing security controls in the prompt.
Ask for prioritized recommendations with clear release blockers and verification steps.
Validate recommendations against official iOS, Android, framework, and app store guidance.
anti_patterns: Do not use it to hide malicious behavior or bypass platform security review.
Do not treat hardening advice as proof that an app is compliant or secure.
Do not request generic advice when architecture, data sensitivity, and platform details are available.

faq.question: Does this skill write mobile app code?
faq.answer: It can guide secure implementation patterns and review code designs, but the skill file itself contains instructions only.

faq.question: Which platforms does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers iOS, Android, React Native, Flutter, Xamarin, Cordova, Unity mobile, and mobile Progressive Web Apps.

faq.question: Can it replace a penetration test?
faq.answer: No. It supports secure design and review, but live testing and expert validation are still required.

faq.question: Does it access Keychain, Keystore, or browser storage?
faq.answer: No. It discusses secure storage concepts but does not execute code or access local secrets.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes legitimate but dual-use hardening topics such as obfuscation, anti-tampering, and dynamic analysis prevention.

faq.question: How should teams use it safely?
faq.answer: Use it for authorized mobile apps, document the threat model, and verify advice with platform documentation and security tests.
